title: Add refunds endpoint and customer transaction history
summary: This PR ships two new endpoints and a refunds service but arrives with a cluster of serious problems across every dimension the team cares about. Security hawk is blocking on PAN exposure in the response body and console.log bypassing pino's PCI redaction. Perf skeptic is blocking on a load-all-then-filter pattern and an N×M scan inside the enrichment loop. Quality critic is blocking on a silent sort bug (wrong field name makes it a no-op), a 200 for not-found, and a test suite whose every assertion passes regardless of correctness. Kiss zealot is blocking on a module-level mutable singleton that the rest of the codebase correctly avoids. There are also meaningful should-level issues: missing pagination, a speculative dollar/cent heuristic nobody asked for, loose amount validation, snake_case/camelCase drift in the response contract, and an unused exported schema. Nothing here is ready to ship.

findings.title: Full card_number field included in API response — potential PAN exposure
findings.message: Line 44 explicitly reaches for `card_number` on the transaction object and forwards it to every caller of this endpoint: `card_number: (tx as unknown as { card_number?: string }).card_number ?? tx.cardLast4`. If the upstream charge flow stored the card number anywhere on the transaction (even accidentally), this serialises it into a JSON response that logs, caches, or forwards it from that moment on. PCI-DSS prohibits PANs from leaving the security boundary in cleartext. The field should be removed entirely — `card_last4` on the same line already provides the display-safe truncated value. The `as unknown as` cast to fish a non-typed field off a domain object is a red flag: you're bypassing the type system specifically to get at data the model doesn't officially carry.

findings.title: console.log bypasses pino redaction — financial data leaks to stdout
findings.message: Lines 25 and 55 both use `console.log` instead of the project's injected pino logger. The project ships a pino logger specifically because it has a redaction allowlist for PCI-sensitive fields — `console.log` routes around that control entirely. Line 55 logs `refund.refundId`, `tx.id`, and `refund.amount` in plaintext. Any log aggregator, crash reporter, or container stdout capture sees this data in full. Use the injected `logger` (`logger.info({ refundId, transactionId, amount }, 'refund created')`) so the redaction config applies uniformly.

findings.title: POST /refunds and GET /customers/:id/transactions have no visible auth guard
findings.message: Both new routes are registered with no authentication or authorization middleware. `POST /refunds` lets any unauthenticated caller issue a refund against any transaction ID they can guess or enumerate. `GET /customers/:id/transactions` exposes the full transaction history of any customer. If auth is handled upstream (API gateway, a global middleware not shown here) please make that explicit in the PR description — right now the diff reads as two wide-open money-movement endpoints. If it is not handled elsewhere, add a middleware that validates the caller's identity and verifies they are entitled to act on the given transaction/customer before these handlers execute.

findings.title: Module-level mutable singleton `refundsStore` — hidden global state
findings.message: `const refundsStore: Record<string, RefundResult> = {}` sits at module scope, making it a process-wide singleton shared across every `RefundsService` instance and across every test that imports this module. The codebase already injects repositories via the constructor — the `InMemoryTransactionRepository` is passed in, not allocated globally. Follow the same pattern: accept a `RefundsStore` (an in-memory map or a proper repository interface) as a constructor argument. Without this, tests that run in the same process bleed state into each other, you can never have two isolated service instances, and there is no way to reset or inspect the store in tests without reaching into the module internals.

findings.title: findAll() + in-memory filter — O(all transactions) on every request
findings.message: Every call to `GET /customers/:id/transactions` loads the entire transaction table into memory and filters in JS (lines 19-26). On dev seed data this is instant. On production with 5M transactions it is a full table scan per support lookup. The fix belongs in the repository: add `findByCustomerId(id: string)` to `TransactionRepository` so the predicate runs at the store level (and eventually on an index when you graduate from the in-memory impl). The comment 'Easy to reason about' does not survive real data volumes.

findings.title: N×M scan: enrichTransactionWithRefunds called in a loop, each call scans all refunds
findings.message: For each of the N transactions that belong to a customer, `enrichTransactionWithRefunds` (line 38) calls `listRefundsForTransaction`, which iterates `Object.keys(refundsStore)` — i.e. *all* refunds — to find the matching ones (refundsService.ts lines 61-67). That is N full scans of the refunds store, making this O(N × M) where N is the customer's transaction count and M is the total number of refunds in the system. Fix: load all refunds for the customer's transaction IDs in a single batched call, then join in memory. Even a simple `groupBy(allRefunds, r => r.transactionId)` after one pass is O(M) instead of O(N×M).

findings.title: No pagination on customer transaction list — unbounded memory growth
findings.message: The `list` handler returns every transaction for a customer with no `limit`, `offset`, or cursor. A customer with years of history returns the entire set in one response and allocates proportional memory on the pod. This is the standard latent-OOM pattern: works in dev, pages someone in prod. Add `limit` (default 50, cap 200) and a cursor or offset parameter. Both the repository interface and this handler need the bounds.

findings.title: HTTP 200 {} returned when transaction is not found — should be 404
findings.message: When `refund()` returns `null` (the transaction doesn't exist), the handler returns `res.status(200).json({})`. A 200 with an empty body is a lie: the caller has no way to distinguish a successful refund from a not-found error without inspecting the body shape — and the body is an empty object, so they can't. Idempotency clients and retry logic will interpret this as success and stop retrying. Return `res.status(404).json({ error: 'transaction not found' })`. If the transaction not existing is already an application-level error thrown by the service, that's even cleaner — throw, and let the error handler produce the 404.

findings.title: Sort key `created_at` doesn't match domain field `createdAt` — sort is a silent no-op
findings.message: Lines 29-31 sort by `a.created_at` and `b.created_at`, but the `Transaction` domain object uses camelCase `createdAt`. Accessing a non-existent property in JS returns `undefined` for every element, so the comparisons (`undefined < undefined`) always return `false` and `undefined > undefined` always returns `false`, making the comparator return 0 for every pair. The sort call produces no reordering — the 'newest-first' guarantee in the PR description is silently broken for every caller. Fix: `(a, b) => b.createdAt.localeCompare(a.createdAt)` (or compare as `Date` objects). Also drop the `as any` casts on the comparator parameters — they hid this exact bug.

findings.title: All three test assertions pass regardless of correctness — test suite proves nothing
findings.message: Every assertion in this file passes no matter what the code does: `expect(refund.body).toBeDefined()` (line 21) is true for any HTTP response including a 500; `expect(refund.status).toBeLessThan(500)` (line 29) passes when the endpoint returns `200 {}` for a missing transaction — exactly the bug on line 22 of refundsController.ts; `expect(res.body).toBeDefined()` (line 37) proves nothing. These are green checks that manufacture false confidence. The first test should assert `expect(refund.status).toBe(201)` and `expect(refund.body).toMatchObject({ transactionId: charge.body.id, amount: 1000, status: 'refunded' })`. The second should assert `expect(refund.status).toBe(404)`. The history test should charge first, then assert the transaction appears in the list with the right shape. Also missing: over-refund rejection, partial refund amount, and input validation error paths.

findings.title: Amount validation accepts zero, negative, and fractional-cent values
findings.message: `amount: z.number().optional()` places no bounds on the refund amount. A caller can send `0`, `-500`, or `9.99` (fractional cents) and the value reaches the service logic unchallenged. The service then uses that amount directly (or silently caps it), with no error surfaced to the caller. Add `.positive()` and `.int()` (assuming amounts are in cents): `amount: z.number().int().positive().optional()`. Validate at the boundary so the service can trust its inputs are sane.

findings.title: Response object mixes snake_case and camelCase — breaks API contract consistency
findings.message: The enriched transaction object emitted by this handler mixes `card_number`, `card_last4`, `customer_id`, `gateway_reference`, `created_at`, `refund_total` (snake_case) alongside `id`, `amount`, `currency`, `status` (camelCase). Separately, `refundsController.ts` lines 27-31 emit `refund_id`, `transaction_id`, `created_at` as snake_case. If the rest of the API uses camelCase (which the charge response appears to), every consumer of these two endpoints needs a special-case transformation. Pick one convention — the house style appears to be camelCase — and apply it consistently. Casing mismatches on sort keys and join predicates are also how silent bugs like the `created_at` vs `createdAt` sort issue slip in.

findings.title: console.log instead of injected pino logger
findings.message: `console.log('history requested for customer ' + customerId)` bypasses the structured logger the rest of the codebase uses. This escapes log-level filtering, log shipping, and the pino redaction allowlist. `customerId` is PII. Use the injected logger: `logger.info({ customerId }, 'customer history requested')` — structured, level-gated, and redaction-safe.

findings.title: Speculative dollar/cent dual-unit heuristic — YAGNI and silently wrong
findings.message: Lines 35-43 contain a heuristic that guesses whether the caller sent an amount in dollars or cents by checking if `refundAmount * 100 <= tx.amount`. This was never asked for, the schema enforces no unit, and the comment 'new clients sometimes send dollars' describes a future integration that doesn't exist yet. The logic also has a silent failure mode: if a caller sends $10.00 meaning ten dollars for a $1500-cent transaction, the heuristic concludes $10 = 1000 cents ≤ 1500 and silently converts — but if they meant 10 cents, they get 10 cents back with no error. Decide on one unit (cents), document it in the schema with a comment and validation, and reject anything that isn't an integer number of cents. Delete this block.

findings.title: historyQuerySchema exported but never used anywhere in the diff
findings.message: `historyQuerySchema` is defined and exported but the customer history controller reads `req.params.id` directly without validating through it. Dead exports: delete it or wire it up. Git remembers; don't leave fossil code on the first commit.
